name: Chinese dynastic trunk: Shang → PRC (12 links, 1600 BCE – present)
description: Twelve-link dynastic trunk of Chinese imperial and post-imperial state forms, from the late Bronze Age Shang at Anyang (traditional dates c. 1600-1046 BCE, archaeologically attested from c. 1250 BCE Anyang phase) through the Zhou, the short brutal Qin unification, the Han consolidation, the Sui reunification after nearly four centuries of fragmentation, the Tang cosmopolitan empire, the Song fiscal-commercial revolution, the Mongol Yuan, the Ming Han-restoration, the Manchu Qing, the Republic of China, and the People's Republic. Every transition is explicitly typed and none is euphemised as mere 'succession': Shang→Zhou and Zhou→Qin were conquest-with-extermination; Qin→Han, Sui→Tang, Yuan→Ming, Qing→ROC, and ROC→PRC were civil-war revolutions; Song→Yuan and Ming→Qing were foreign conquests by Inner Asian powers; only Tang→Song (via the bloodless 960 Chenqiao mutiny) and Han→Sui (a 360-year fragmentation bridge in which no single heir survives) approach 'succession', and even those are flagged with caveats.
ethical_notes: Six structural points of care. (a) The chain is a 'trunk' — it deliberately omits dozens of simultaneous states (Three Kingdoms: Wei/Shu/Wu 220-280; Sixteen Kingdoms; Northern & Southern Dynasties 420-589; Liao 907-1125, Jin 1115-1234, Western Xia 1038-1227 during Song; Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms 907-960) whose rulers would contest the 'main line' framing. Representing imperial China as a single unbroken lineage is a Qing-era historiographical construct (the Erchen zhuan and the Twenty-Four Histories genre), and users should understand that every 'succession' here papers over multi-state reality. (b) The 360-year gap between Han's fall (220 CE) and Sui reunification (581 CE) contains tens of millions of deaths (Three Kingdoms population collapse alone is estimated at 50-60 million, from ~60M to ~16M census-registered by 280 CE) — the trunk compresses this to a single SUCCESSION arrow and that is a major silence. (c) The An Lushan Rebellion (755-763, within the Tang link) is probably the single deadliest event in the chain not marked as a transition: census comparison between 754 and 764 shows a loss of ~36 million registered persons, much of it war-driven disruption of tax registers but with catastrophic real mortality. (d) Mongol conquest of the Jin (1211-1234), the Western Xia (1205-1227), and Song (1235-1279) killed an estimated 30-60 million in northern and central China; the Yuan→Ming transition (Red Turban Rebellion and famine, 1351-1368) killed perhaps another 30 million. These are not side-events; they are among the deadliest wars in pre-industrial human history. (e) The Manchu conquest of Ming (1644-1662) included the Yangzhou massacre (1645, estimates 80,000-800,000 dead, eyewitness account in Wang Xiuchu's 揚州十日記), the Jiading three-massacres, and the queue-edict (tifayi) which imposed the Manchu hairstyle on penalty of death — conquest with forced bodily assimilation. (f) The 1949 founding of the PRC is correctly marked REVOLUTION but carries forward massive subsequent violence not captured by the transition year itself: Great Leap Famine (1959-1961, ~15-45 million dead, Dikötter 2010 / Yang Jisheng 2012), Cultural Revolution (1966-1976, estimates 500,000-2 million killed, tens of millions persecuted), 1989 Tiananmen suppression. The trunk records the founding moment; users must consult event-level records for the regime-level violence that follows. Academic consensus across Fairbank, Twitchett (Cambridge History of China, 15 vols.), Spence, Dikötter, and Chinese-language scholarship (Ge Jianxiong's demographic work) is reflected throughout; disputed dates for Shang (traditional 1600 BCE vs. archaeological c. 1250 BCE Anyang) are noted at the entity level.

sources.citation: Twitchett, D., Fairbank, J.K. (gen. eds.) (1978-2020). The Cambridge History of China, 15 volumes. Cambridge UP.
Spence, J.D. (1990). The Search for Modern China. Norton.
Ebrey, P.B. (2010). The Cambridge Illustrated History of China. 2nd ed. Cambridge UP.
Ge Jianxiong 葛劍雄 (ed.) (2000). 中國人口史 (Demographic History of China), 6 vols. Fudan UP.
Wang Xiuchu 王秀楚 (1645, pub. 19th c.). 揚州十日記 (Ten Days' Record of Yangzhou) — eyewitness account of the 1645 massacre.
Sima Qian 司馬遷 (c. 94 BCE). 史記 (Shiji / Records of the Grand Historian) — primary source for Shang through early Han dynastic succession.
Dikötter, F. (2010). Mao's Great Famine. Walker.
Yang Jisheng 楊繼繩 (2008, Eng. 2012). Tombstone: The Great Chinese Famine. Farrar, Straus and Giroux.

links.description: Shang dynasty — the earliest Chinese state directly attested by contemporary written sources (oracle-bone inscriptions from Yinxu, the late Shang capital at modern Anyang). Traditional dates 1600-1046 BCE; archaeological Anyang phase begins c. 1250 BCE. The Shang were a bronze-casting ritual kingship centred on divination, royal ancestor worship, and chariot warfare, ruling a core territory on the Central Plain (中原) with tributary relations to surrounding Bronze Age polities.
links.ethical_notes: Shang religious practice included systematic human sacrifice: Anyang excavations have uncovered sacrificial pits with thousands of victims (often war captives from the Qiang people to the west). Early narratives in the Shiji and the Book of Documents (Shangshu) depicting Shang as a cruel tyranny under the last king Zhou 紂 are Zhou-era political propaganda retrospectively justifying the conquest; modern scholarship (Keightley, Chang) treats the propaganda with skepticism while acknowledging the sacrificial-violence substrate as archaeologically real.

links.description: The Zhou conquest: King Wu of Zhou 周武王 defeats the last Shang king Di Xin 帝辛 (pejoratively called Zhou 紂) at the Battle of Muye 牧野之戰 (traditional date 1046 BCE, confirmed astronomically by the Xia-Shang-Zhou Chronology Project 1996-2000). Di Xin self-immolates at his Luhai pavilion. The Zhou establish twin capitals at Fenghao (west) and Luoyi (east), proclaim the Mandate of Heaven (天命) doctrine justifying dynastic replacement, and enfeoff Zhou kinsmen and allied lineages in a proto-feudal system that will gradually fragment into the Spring-Autumn (770-476 BCE) and Warring States (475-221 BCE) periods.
links.ethical_notes: The Mandate of Heaven rhetoric frames Shang as corrupt and deserving overthrow; this is the founding ideological move of Chinese imperial historiography and sets the template for all subsequent dynastic transitions (every new dynasty will claim its predecessor lost the Mandate). Modern archaeology (oracle bones, bronzes) shows the late Shang was a sophisticated ritual kingship, not the debauched tyranny of the Zhou narrative. The conquest included systematic elimination of Shang royal kin and mass resettlement of Shang populations to enfeoffed Zhou states.

links.description: Qin Shi Huang 秦始皇帝 completes the unification of the Warring States with the elimination of the last rival state, Qi, in 221 BCE, after serial conquests of Han (230), Zhao (228), Wei (225), Chu (223), and Yan (222). The Zhou royal house had been deposed in 256 BCE; the Qin state had grown under Legalist reforms (Shang Yang, 4th c. BCE) to dominate the other six. Qin Shi Huang declares the first imperial title (皇帝 huangdi), standardises script, weights, measures, and axle-widths, and commissions the initial Great Wall linkage and the terracotta necropolis at Mount Li.
links.ethical_notes: The Qin unification was extraordinarily violent: Bai Qi's 白起 campaign against Zhao culminated in the Changping massacre (260 BCE, estimates of 400,000 Zhao prisoners buried alive — figure from Sima Qian, debated by modern historians but a real mass atrocity). The Qin standardisation was imposed by terror: Li Si's 焚書坑儒 book-burning (213 BCE) and scholar-execution (212 BCE, traditional 460 Confucian scholars buried alive) targeted intellectual pluralism. Forced labour on the Great Wall, the Epang Palace, and the Mount Li necropolis caused mass mortality — census estimates of Qin mobilisation suggest ~15% of adult males conscripted simultaneously. The Qin collapse (209-206 BCE, triggered by the Chen Sheng–Wu Guang peasant uprising) followed directly from this overextraction.

links.description: The Chu-Han Contention 楚漢之爭 (206-202 BCE): Liu Bang 劉邦 (Gaozu 高祖), a commoner and former minor Qin official, defeats the aristocratic Chu commander Xiang Yu 項羽 at the Battle of Gaixia 垓下之戰 (January 202 BCE). Xiang Yu's suicide at the Wu River and Liu Bang's enthronement as emperor at Dingtao found the Han dynasty. The Western Han (202 BCE – 9 CE) and Eastern Han (25-220 CE), interrupted by Wang Mang's Xin interregnum (9-23 CE), consolidate the imperial system the Qin had violently imposed, softening Legalist autocracy with Confucian ritual and bureaucratic selection. The self-designation 漢 (Han) becomes the canonical ethnonym for the majority Chinese population.
links.ethical_notes: The Han revolution is a peasant-led replacement of a collapsed Qin autocracy; it is genuine social mobility (Liu Bang had been a minor pavilion chief, 亭長). But the Han consolidation included the extermination of early allied kings (Han Xin 韓信 and others purged in the 190s BCE) and prolonged frontier wars against the Xiongnu (under Han Wudi, r. 141-87 BCE: Huo Qubing and Wei Qing campaigns, mass cavalry mobilisations with ~90% horse-loss rates per the Shiji). The Han-canonical identity is itself a political construction — the conflation of 'Han' with 'Chinese' erases dozens of non-Han peoples within the empire's claimed boundaries (the Baiyue of the south, the Qiang in the west, the Xiongnu successor peoples in the north).

links.description: Yang Jian 楊堅 (Emperor Wen of Sui 隋文帝) usurps the Northern Zhou throne from his seven-year-old grandson Emperor Jing in 581, declares the Sui dynasty, and by 589 reunifies China with the conquest of the Chen dynasty in the south — ending the 360-year fragmentation that followed Han's collapse in 220 CE. The Sui is short-lived (581-618) but institutionally foundational: the Grand Canal (Yangdi's forced-labour project, 605-609, ~5 million conscripted, mass mortality), the keju imperial examination system, the equal-field land system, and the administrative template that Tang will inherit.
links.ethical_notes: The Sui succession is flagged SUCCESSION rather than CONQUEST only because Yang Jian's coup was an intra-dynastic usurpation within the Northern Zhou house; the REAL transition this link compresses is the 361-year gap since Han (220-581 CE) containing the Three Kingdoms (Wei/Shu/Wu), the Jin dynasty, the Sixteen Kingdoms period, and the Northern & Southern Dynasties. Population-registered persons fell from ~60 million (Han census, 2 CE) to ~16 million (Western Jin census, 280 CE) — a collapse of ~75%, among the largest relative demographic contractions in world history. The trunk chain's single-link compression of this period is a severe historiographical silence; detailed treatment requires the event-level and entity-level records, not this trunk. The Sui's own Grand Canal project killed perhaps 1-2 million conscripts and triggered the rebellions that destroyed the dynasty.

links.description: Li Yuan 李淵 (Emperor Gaozu of Tang 唐高祖), a regional Sui military governor (Taiyuan), leads a rebellion against the increasingly erratic Emperor Yang of Sui 隋煬帝 in 617. After Yangdi is assassinated by his own guard at Jiangdu in 618, Li Yuan takes the throne at Chang'an and founds the Tang. His son Li Shimin 李世民 — later Emperor Taizong 唐太宗, the Tang's greatest ruler — consolidates power in the Xuanwu Gate Incident (626) by killing his two brothers and forcing his father's abdication. The Tang (618-907) becomes the most cosmopolitan empire in Chinese history, with Chang'an as a million-person capital hosting Sogdian, Persian, Arab, Korean, Japanese, and Uighur communities; the Silk Road operates at its medieval peak.
links.ethical_notes: The Tang founding was violent (Xuanwu Gate fratricide; suppression of Sui loyalists). More catastrophic: the An Lushan Rebellion (755-763), when the Sogdian-Turkic general An Lushan 安祿山 revolted from Fanyang, captured Chang'an and Luoyang, and destroyed the Tang fiscal-administrative system. Census comparison 754→764 shows a loss of ~36 million registered persons — probably the deadliest event in pre-industrial Chinese history, with massive real mortality in the Central Plain compounded by the collapse of tax registration. Tang never fully recovered; the remaining 150 years (763-907) were a slow fiscal and military decline into the Five Dynasties. The Tang cosmopolitan image (often cited to depict imperial China as open and multicultural) is historically real but coexisted with the Huichang Buddhist persecution of 845 and the Canton massacre of foreign merchants during the Huang Chao rebellion (879, estimated 120,000 Arab, Persian, and Jewish merchants killed).

links.description: The Chenqiao Mutiny 陳橋兵變: Zhao Kuangyin 趙匡胤, senior general of the Later Zhou, is acclaimed emperor by his troops at Chenqiao (modern Fengqiu, Henan) on 3 February 960 while nominally marching north to repel a Khitan incursion. The young Later Zhou emperor Gongdi (seven years old) abdicates without bloodshed; Zhao takes the throne as Emperor Taizu of Song 宋太祖. Over the next two decades Taizu and his brother Taizong reunify most of China proper, ending the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms fragmentation (907-960). The Song (960-1279, Northern Song 960-1127 and Southern Song 1127-1279) will be the commercial and cultural apex of pre-modern East Asia: paper money, gunpowder weapons, the printing-press at scale, Neo-Confucian philosophy (Zhu Xi), and the maturation of the keju examination system.
links.ethical_notes: The Chenqiao mutiny itself was genuinely bloodless — the 'Yellow Robe' acclamation and the child emperor's peaceful abdication are why this link is marked is_violent=false even though the SURROUNDING fifty-year period (907-960 Five Dynasties) was among the most violent interludes in Chinese history, with five northern dynasties and ten regional kingdoms in chronic civil war. The trunk chain compresses all of this into a single transition moment; users should understand that 'Tang → Song succession' is a five-decade fragmentation that the trunk erases. Additionally: the Song did NOT control northern China — the Liao (Khitan, 907-1125) held the Sixteen Prefectures including Yanjing (modern Beijing); the Jin (Jurchen, 1115-1234) conquered Northern Song in 1127 (Jingkang Incident, the two captured Song emperors died in Jurchen exile); Western Xia (Tangut, 1038-1227) held the northwest. The Song is one of four simultaneous states through most of its history — the 'Chinese' trunk framing is profoundly misleading for this period.

links.description: Kublai Khan 忽必烈 proclaims the Yuan dynasty 大元 in 1271 at Khanbaliq (modern Beijing), choosing a Chinese-style dynastic name from the Yijing ('origin of heaven'). The Mongol conquest is completed with the fall of the Southern Song capital Lin'an (modern Hangzhou) in 1276 and the final extinction of Song resistance at the naval battle of Yamen 崖山海戰 (19 March 1279), where Lu Xiufu 陸秀夫 leaps into the sea with the seven-year-old last Song emperor Zhao Bing 趙昺 in his arms. The Yuan (1271-1368) is the first foreign conquest dynasty to rule all of China proper; Khanbaliq becomes a hub of trans-Eurasian exchange (Marco Polo's visit 1271-1295; Nestorian Christian and Tibetan Buddhist court activity).
links.ethical_notes: The Mongol conquests of North China (Jin, 1211-1234), Western Xia (1205-1227), Dali (1253), and Southern Song (1235-1279) killed an estimated 30-60 million people — the single largest episode of mass mortality in medieval world history. The Xixia (Tangut) population was exterminated so thoroughly that the Tangut script became undecipherable until the 20th century (Kozlov's 1909 Khara-Khoto finds enabled reconstruction). The Yuan institutionalised a four-caste system (Mongols, Semu or 'colour-eyed' Central Asians, Han-ren or northern Chinese, Nan-ren or southern Chinese) with legal and tax discrimination against the Chinese majority. Yamen 1279 is marked in Chinese historiography (notably Qian Mu's Outline of National History 國史大綱) as the end of 'Chinese civilisation in the classical sense' — a framing that is itself a Han-nationalist interpretive choice that the trunk chain flags without endorsing.

links.description: Zhu Yuanzhang 朱元璋, a destitute Buddhist novice and former beggar who rose through the Red Turban rebellions (紅巾軍) against the Yuan, proclaims the Ming dynasty 大明 at Yingtianfu (modern Nanjing) on 23 January 1368. His Northern Expedition under Xu Da 徐達 captures Khanbaliq on 14 September 1368; the last Yuan emperor Toghon Temür 妥懽帖睦爾 flees to Shangdu, then to Karakorum, ending Mongol rule in China proper. Zhu takes the reign-name Hongwu 洪武. The Ming (1368-1644) restores Han rule, rebuilds the Grand Canal and the Great Wall in its current stone form, launches Zheng He's treasure-voyages (1405-1433) to the Indian Ocean, and builds the Forbidden City (Yongle reign, completed 1420).
links.ethical_notes: The Ming founding narrative emphasises restoration of Han rule after 'barbarian' Yuan; in reality Zhu Yuanzhang's revolution was a peasant uprising driven by Yuan-era famine, plague (the 1330s-40s pandemics that also produced the European Black Death), and fiscal collapse. Hongwu's consolidation was extraordinarily bloody: the Hu Weiyong case (1380, ~30,000 executed over 15 years) eliminated the chancellorate; the Lan Yu case (1393, ~15,000 executed) destroyed the military high command; the literary inquisition (文字獄) persecuted Ming-era scholars for suspected Yuan loyalism. Hongwu built autocratic imperial power unprecedented since Qin. The Ming's demographic recovery from Yuan collapse (population perhaps doubled 1380→1600) was real, but the founding decades were a peasant-autocrat's reign of terror.

links.description: The Manchu conquest: Beijing falls first to the peasant-rebel Li Zicheng 李自成 on 25 April 1644 (the Chongzhen emperor hangs himself on Coal Hill); the Ming general Wu Sangui 吳三桂, commanding the Shanhai Pass garrison, allies with the Manchu regent Dorgon 多爾袞 rather than submit to Li, and Manchu forces enter Beijing on 5 June 1644 under the child Shunzhi emperor. Manchu consolidation takes eighteen more years: the Southern Ming court at Nanjing falls 1645, at Fuzhou 1646, at Zhaoqing 1651; Koxinga's 鄭成功 resistance holds Taiwan from 1661 until 1683. The Qing 大清帝國 (1636 Shenyang proclamation; 1644 Beijing entry; 1912 Xuantong abdication) will be the largest Chinese empire in territorial extent, incorporating Mongolia, Tibet, Xinjiang (Qianlong's Zunghar conquest 1755-1759, estimated 80% Zunghar population death by massacre and smallpox), and Taiwan.
links.ethical_notes: The Manchu conquest included systematic civilian massacres: Yangzhou 1645 (eyewitness account in Wang Xiuchu's 揚州十日記, estimates 80,000-800,000 killed over ten days); the Jiading three-massacres 嘉定三屠 of 1645; Guangzhou 1650. The queue-edict 剃髮令 (1645) imposed the Manchu hairstyle on all adult Han men on penalty of death — 'keep your hair and lose your head, keep your head and lose your hair' (留髮不留頭，留頭不留髮). This is conquest-with-forced-bodily-assimilation, a fact muted in later Qing historiography which re-framed the dynasty as a naturalised Chinese imperial house. The Zunghar genocide of 1755-1759 under Qianlong is attested in Qing sources themselves and is one of the most complete ethnic exterminations in early-modern history (Perdue, China Marches West, 2005). The Qing late period will see the Taiping Civil War (1851-1864, 20-70 million dead — deadliest civil war in human history), the Dungan revolts, the Opium Wars (1839-1842, 1856-1860), and the colonial concessions — the trunk records only the 1644 founding, not the subsequent century of collapse.

links.description: The Xinhai Revolution 辛亥革命: the Wuchang Uprising of 10 October 1911 triggers the defection of province after province from Qing rule. Sun Yat-sen 孫中山 returns from exile and is inaugurated Provisional President of the Republic of China at Nanjing on 1 January 1912. Yuan Shikai 袁世凱, commander of the Qing Beiyang Army, negotiates the abdication of the six-year-old Xuantong emperor Puyi 溥儀 (12 February 1912) in exchange for the presidency. 2,132 years of imperial rule (since Qin Shi Huang 221 BCE) end. The ROC (1912-1949 on the mainland; 1949-present on Taiwan) passes through the Yuan Shikai monarchy attempt (1915-16), the Warlord Era (1916-1928), the Nanjing Decade under Chiang Kai-shek (1928-1937), the Second Sino-Japanese War (1937-1945), and the Chinese Civil War (1927-1937, 1945-1949).
links.ethical_notes: The Xinhai Revolution's founding rhetoric was explicitly Han-nationalist: Sun Yat-sen's Tongmenghui slogan '驅除韃虜，恢復中華' ('expel the Tartar barbarians, restore China') framed the Qing as alien colonial rule. Early-Republic anti-Manchu pogroms killed thousands (Xi'an massacre, October 1911; Taiyuan massacre). Manchu banner communities were disbanded, pensioned off, or massacred. Tibet (under the 13th Dalai Lama) and Outer Mongolia (under the Bogd Khan) declared independence, taking advantage of Qing collapse; the ROC's subsequent military campaigns to reclaim both territories would continue through the 1940s. The Republic's constitutional structure was chronically fragile; real power in 1912-1928 lay with regional warlord armies, and the Nanjing Decade depended on an alliance of convenience between Chiang's Kuomintang, Shanghai bankers, and Green Gang organised crime.

links.description: The Chinese Communist Party victory in the Chinese Civil War: after the failure of the Marshall Mission's mediation (1945-46), open civil war resumes with Chiang Kai-shek's Manchuria offensive. The Liao-Shen (September–November 1948), Huai-Hai (November 1948 – January 1949), and Ping-Jin (November 1948 – January 1949) campaigns destroy Nationalist main forces. Beiping (soon renamed Beijing) falls 31 January 1949; Nanjing 23 April; Shanghai 27 May. Mao Zedong 毛澤東 proclaims the People's Republic of China from Tiananmen on 1 October 1949; Chiang Kai-shek retreats to Taiwan with ~2 million followers in December 1949. The PRC (1949-present) reunifies China proper, launches Land Reform (1949-1953, ~2 million landlords killed), the Korean War intervention (1950-1953), the Anti-Rightist Campaign (1957), the Great Leap Forward and its famine (1958-1961, 15-45 million dead), the Cultural Revolution (1966-1976), the Reform and Opening under Deng Xiaoping (1978-), and the Xi Jinping consolidation (2012-).
links.ethical_notes: The 1949 founding is marked REVOLUTION; the accompanying violence is extensive and ongoing. Civil War dead (1945-1949): estimates 2-6 million combat deaths, millions more from famine and displacement. Land Reform (1949-1953): ~2 million landlords killed. The Great Leap Famine (1959-1961): Yang Jisheng's Tombstone documents ~36 million deaths; Dikötter's estimate is 45 million; Chinese official scholarship acknowledges 15-20 million 'non-normal deaths'. Cultural Revolution (1966-1976): estimates 500,000-2 million killed, ~100 million persecuted; the Guangxi cannibalism incidents (1967-1968) are documented in internal CCP investigations later leaked (Zheng Yi, Scarlet Memorial). Tiananmen suppression (3-4 June 1989): hundreds to low thousands killed. Xinjiang detention system (2017-present): estimates 1-1.8 million Uyghurs and other Turkic Muslims in 're-education' camps (Zenz, UHRP, Associated Press 2020). Hong Kong's 2019-2020 democracy protests and the 2020 National Security Law imposition. The PRC is the canonical successor state to the ROC on the mainland; it is simultaneously responsible for the largest famine in human history and the fastest large-scale poverty reduction (800 million lifted out of absolute poverty 1978-2020 per World Bank data). Both facts are real; neither should erase the other.
